claims.claim_text: nginx is the world's most popular web server.
claims.span_hint: section: What is a Web Server?
claims.rationale: The official nginx site makes this claim, but `nginx.org` is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: nginx powers about a third of all websites on the internet.
claims.span_hint: section: What is a Web Server?
claims.rationale: This market-share figure is verifiable from a live web-server-usage tracker, but the primary source is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Docker Desktop is free for personal use and qualifying small businesses.
claims.rationale: Docker's pricing page directly states Docker Personal includes Docker Desktop at no cost for personal use and qualifying small businesses.

claims.claim_text: OrbStack is the fastest, lightest, best-UX option on macOS.
claims.rationale: These are unbenchmarked superlatives and subjective rankings, not stable facts.

claims.claim_text: OrbStack is free for personal use.
claims.rationale: OrbStack's pricing page states this directly, but `orbstack.dev` is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Podman is daemonless and rootless by default.
claims.rationale: Podman documentation directly describes Podman as daemonless and documents rootless operation, but the docs host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Rancher Desktop includes built-in Kubernetes.
claims.rationale: Rancher Desktop documentation says Kubernetes is built in, but the official docs host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Using `alias docker=podman` is a valid compatibility shortcut.
claims.span_hint: section: Option A > Step 1 Linux notes
claims.rationale: Podman documentation explicitly says `alias docker=podman`, but that primary source is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A successful install should show `Docker version 24.x.x` or newer.
claims.anchor_text: You should see something like `Docker version 24.x.x` or newer.
claims.suggested_rewrite: You should see output that begins with `Docker version`.
claims.rationale: The specific version floor will age quickly; the teaching point is simply that Docker prints a version string.

claims.claim_text: Port 80 is the default port for HTTP web traffic.
claims.rationale: MDN's HTTP session guide states that the default port for HTTP servers is port 80.

claims.claim_text: The default web root in the official nginx container image is `/usr/share/nginx/html/`.
claims.rationale: The Docker Official Image documentation states that nginx serves files from `/usr/share/nginx/html` by default.

claims.claim_text: Oracle Cloud has the most generous free tier.
claims.rationale: This is a comparative superlative without a stable benchmark.

claims.claim_text: Oracle Cloud Free Tier includes always-free compute instances.
claims.rationale: Oracle's Free Tier FAQ lists AMD-based and Ampere A1 compute under Always Free services.

claims.claim_text: Google Cloud offers $300 in free credit for 90 days.
claims.rationale: Google Cloud's Free Program documentation directly describes the 90-day, $300 Free Trial.

claims.claim_text: AWS free-tier VM eligibility depends on whether the account was created before or after July 15, 2025, and the eligible instance types differ by cohort.
claims.span_hint: section: Option B > provider list and Step 1
claims.rationale: AWS EC2 documentation explicitly separates pre- and post-July 15, 2025 account cohorts and lists the free-tier-eligible instance types for each.

claims.claim_text: Ubuntu's default nginx document root is `/var/www/html/`, not `/var/share/`.
claims.rationale: Ubuntu's nginx tutorial identifies `/var/www/html/` as the default page location, but `ubuntu.com` is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The first website is still available at its original URL.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? > first website bullet
claims.rationale: CERN's first-website page states that the first website is available at its original URL.

claims.claim_text: Tim Berners-Lee created the first website in 1991 at CERN.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? > first website bullet
claims.anchor_text: Tim Berners-Lee created it in 1991 at CERN.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Tim Berners-Lee created the first website at CERN during the Web's earliest days.
claims.rationale: CERN's own history places the first Web server and browser in 1990, so the year in the module is too specific and not safely supportable as written.

claims.claim_text: The first website was served from a NeXT computer with a handwritten 'This machine is a server. DO NOT POWER IT DOWN!!' note.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? > first website bullet
claims.rationale: CERN's short history of the Web page documents both the NeXT machine and the handwritten warning label.

claims.claim_text: Igor Sysoev began developing nginx in 2002 to address the C10K problem of handling 10,000 simultaneous connections.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? > nginx history bullet
claims.rationale: The official NGINX history post covers the 2002 origin and C10K motivation, but the source host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: nginx became known for an event-driven architecture and, with tuning, can handle hundreds of thousands of simultaneous connections.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? > nginx history bullet
claims.rationale: The official NGINX engineering write-up directly supports the architecture and high-concurrency claim, but the host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Netflix, Airbnb, and Dropbox all use nginx as their web server, and the learner's setup is fundamentally the same technology.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? > company usage bullet
claims.anchor_text: **Your website is served the same way Netflix is.** Seriously. Netflix, Airbnb, and Dropbox all use nginx as their web server. The difference between your setup and theirs is scale (they have thousands of servers) and configuration (they have teams of engineers tweaking settings). But the fundamental technology -- a process listening on port 80 and returning HTML -- is identical.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Large-scale websites use the same basic pattern.** A server process listens for HTTP requests and returns content, even though production systems add far more servers and configuration around it.
claims.rationale: Named-company stack claims are time-sensitive and unnecessary to preserve the teaching point.

further_reading.title: A typical HTTP session
further_reading.why_relevant: It gives a beginner-friendly explanation of how browsers, TCP, HTTP requests, and responses fit together.

further_reading.title: nginx Docker Official Image overview
further_reading.why_relevant: It shows how the official nginx container is run and where it serves content from by default.

further_reading.title: The birth of the Web
further_reading.why_relevant: It connects the learner's first-server exercise to the real origin story of the Web at CERN.

notes: Quiz items, quiz answers, exercise steps, and the Mermaid diagram were intentionally excluded per the prompt; this seed covers narrative claims, vendor/tool facts, pricing/free-tier facts, and historical anecdotes.
